title: The Oberbaumbrücke: Quick Summary
items: **The Oberbaumbrücke is Berlin's most photographed bridge**, a double-decker of red brick over the Spree that links Kreuzberg and Friedrichshain. The U1 and U3 run across the top; the road, bike lane and walkways run below.
**It looks medieval but opened in 1896.** The two 34-metre towers copy an old Brandenburg city gate, the work of architect Otto Stahn, so the whole bridge reads much older than it is.

**The old East-West border ran across the water here.** From 1961 the bridge sat on the frontier between the American sector (Kreuzberg) and the Soviet sector (Friedrichshain), and from December 1963 it was a foot crossing for West Berliners only.

**It reopened on 9 November 1994**, five years to the day after the Wall fell, with a steel middle section by Santiago Calatrava replacing the span the Wehrmacht blew up in April 1945. The U-Bahn returned across it in 1995.
**Go at dusk.** The brick glows in late light, and from nightfall until about 1am a neon "Stone, Paper, Scissors" game plays in the arch over the river.

**Combine it in one short walk.** The East Side Gallery runs along the riverbank to the bridge, the Molecule Man stands in the water just upstream, and Warschauer Straße (U1, U3, S-Bahn, M10 tram) is minutes away.
